title: Rheumatoid factor
body_html:
Background
  • Rheumatoid factors (RFs) are autoantibodies directed against the Fc portion of IgG.
  • Most commonly IgM, but can also be IgG or IgA.

Mechanism
  • RF binds to IgG, forming immune complexes.
  • These deposit in joints and tissues, activating complement and driving inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis (RA).
  • Not a direct inflammatory marker (like CRP/ESR), but part of the autoimmune process.

Clinical use
Diagnostic marker:
  • Positive in ~70–80% of patients with RA (but not specific).
Also positive in:
  • Sjögren’s syndrome
  • SLE
  • Chronic infections (e.g. hepatitis C, TB, subacute bacterial endocarditis)
  • Sometimes in healthy elderly individuals

Prognostic marker:
  • High titres of RF are associated with more severe disease, extra-articular manifestations, and worse outcomes in RA.

RF is not for disease activity monitoring, ESR/CRP are used instead.

Testing
  • Usually detected by nephelometry, ELISA, or latex agglutination assays.
  • Reported as positive/negative or in titre levels.

Related autoantibodies
Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies):
  • More specific for RA (~95%).
  • Can appear earlier than RF.
  • Often used alongside RF for diagnosis and prognosis.

Key Points
  • RF is positive in approximately 70% of individuals with Rheumatoid arthritis and 5% of the unaffected population.
  • RF is an antibody (IgM) directed against IgG (Fc Portion)
  • High titres are associated with severe RA but RF not used to monitor inflammation
  • Anti-CCP (cyclic citrullinated peptide) is preferred as a test for rheumatoid arthritis as it is present in 90-95% of the rheumatoid population
